title: Pizzelle
subtitle: Thin, crisp Italian waffle biscuits flavoured with anise or vanilla.
excerpt: Pizzelle are thin, crisp Italian biscuits made by pressing a simple egg batter in a hot iron. They cool to a wafer-like crispness within a minute of leaving the iron and keep for weeks in a tin. The classic flavour is anise, but vanilla works just as well. Makes about 24.
sourceNotes: Pizzelle are one of the oldest known Italian biscuits, originating in the Abruzzo region. The word comes from the Italian pizze, meaning small, flat, and round. The traditional iron bears a snowflake or flower pattern. Pizzelle are associated with Italian holidays, particularly Christmas and Easter, and with celebrations. Italian immigrants brought the tradition to the United States, where the biscuits became well known in Italian-American communities. The batter is a simple mix of eggs, sugar, fat, flour, and flavouring, pressed in a hot hinged iron to produce a thin, embossed biscuit.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer between sheets of baking paper. They are fragile, so pack carefully. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The batter doubles well. Bake in batches, keeping finished pizzelle in a single layer on a rack.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pizzelle keep in a sealed tin for up to three weeks in dry conditions.
recipe.temperatureNote: No oven needed. Cook on a pizzelle or waffle iron.

recipeTools.notes: A pizzelle iron is a thin, patterned version of a waffle iron. A standard waffle iron works but gives a thicker result.

content.text: Pizzelle batter is thin and pourable. A small amount (about a tablespoon) goes onto the centre of the hot iron, the lid closes, and within 30 to 45 seconds you have a biscuit. They are soft for the first minute off the iron and then crisp very quickly. Work fast: if you want to shape them into cones or tubes, do so while they are still warm.

items.prepNote: use instead of anise, or both

content.text: Whisk {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until combined. Whisk in {{unsalted-butter}}.
Add {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}} and stir to a smooth batter. Stir in {{anise-seeds}} or {{vanilla-extract}}, or both.
Heat the pizzelle iron or waffle iron according to the manufacturer's instructions. Lightly brush the plates with a little melted butter before the first pizzella.

Place a heaped tablespoon of batter in the centre of the bottom plate. Close the iron and cook for 30 to 45 seconds until the steam stops. Open the iron and check: the pizzella should be pale golden. If it sticks, leave for another 10 seconds.

Lift with a thin spatula and transfer to a cooling rack. It will be flexible for about 30 seconds and then crisp hard. Lay flat unless you want to roll it into a cone or tube shape, which must be done while still warm.
Continue with the remaining batter. You do not need to re-grease the iron between each pizzella unless they begin to stick.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Pizzelle are fast and straightforward once the iron is up to temperature.

items.symptom: Pizzelle soft rather than crisp after cooling.
items.cause: Undercooked, or stored before fully cool.
items.fix: Cook a few seconds longer; they should be pale golden, not white. Store only once completely cold and hard.

items.cause: Iron not hot enough, or not enough butter on the first use.
items.fix: Let the iron heat for at least 5 minutes before starting. Brush lightly with butter at the first sign of sticking.
